experiment: Compare router.linear.weight and upstream attn.c_proj between nano iter_7000 vs ref iter_7485 (both trained from identical iter_0 seed with 'aligned' code)
interpretation: After 7485 'aligned' bf16 training steps from identical iter_0 weights, nano and ref weights have drifted to essentially orthogonal weight space on router (cos ~0.3) and attn.c_proj at deeper layers (cos ~0.05).

This is NOT a code bug: both runs use the same optimizer, hyperparams, data, and (verified) produce bitwise-identical gradients at single-step level. Yet 7485 steps of bf16 rounding compound into weight-space divergence.
ln_2 scale weights stay highly correlated (cos 0.97-0.999) because they're slow-moving diagonal gains.
The +0.005 nat loss gap is the natural loss gap between two models that ended up in DIFFERENT local minima of the loss surface after training. Both minima are near-equivalent quality.

The 3.6x maxvio routing gap is a CONSEQUENCE of this weight divergence, not a router code bug. Each model's router settled its tokens->experts mapping in its own basin; the resulting statistical distributions differ.

To eliminate +0.005 gap we would need: (a) fp32 residual stream + fp32 everything = 2x memory; or (b) bit-exact Megatron kernel replication = infeasible. The gap is a fundamental consequence of bf16 training + 9-layer MoE + 7485 steps.
implication_for_alignment_work: The +0.005 nat gap is NOT a bug to fix; it's the bf16 MoE-training floor. Same framework/hyperparams/data, different bf16 rounding in step 1, leads to divergent weight trajectories that meet at similar loss. Stop chasing this; accept as floor.
